claim: Context engineering refers to strategies for curating and maintaining the optimal set of tokens during LLM inference, including documents, tools, memory, instructions, domain knowledge, and conversation history.
actionable: When building AI agents, explicitly manage all sources of context (docs, tools, memory, instructions) rather than treating prompting as the only lever.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents

claim: Language models degrade in performance as context window grows beyond ~40-100k tokens, contradicting vendor marketing of million-token windows - this is called context rot.
actionable: Don't assume large context windows solve the problem; test your actual performance degradation empirically and expect severe falloff around 40-100k tokens.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents

claim: LLMs are poor at negative examples ('don't do X') and excel with positive examples - showing the desired behavior is 1000x more effective than prohibition.
actionable: Replace negative instructions in system prompts with 2-3 positive few-shot examples demonstrating the exact desired output format or tone.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents

claim: Most AI product failures are now context failures, not reasoning failures - models like Opus are capable enough; the bottleneck is curating the right information for the task.
actionable: When an agent underperforms, first audit what context it received (system prompt, tools, message history, retrieved documents) before assuming the model isn't smart enough.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents

claim: Use routing and prompt chaining (simple workflows) instead of agentic loops for most business automation - tool-calling loops are only necessary when the agent must truly decide which action to take next.
actionable: Default to deterministic workflows with routing; only adopt agentic loops if you genuinely need the agent to explore multiple possible paths autonomously.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents

claim: Integrate tracing tools like Langfuse early to see the full conversation tree (system prompt, messages, tool calls) - this almost always reveals the root cause of agent failures immediately.
actionable: Set up Langfuse or equivalent tracing in development before shipping; view the complete trace when the agent underperforms rather than trying to debug from test output.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents

claim: Break large complex prompts into sub-problems with a router agent that decides which path to take - this is more reliable than stuffing everything into one huge prompt with if/else statements.
actionable: When your system prompt exceeds 1000 tokens of conditional logic, split into router + specialized sub-agents instead of expanding the main prompt.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents

claim: For RAG, avoid putting entire documents in context; use retrieval with reranking (retrieve 50 chunks broadly, rerank to get top 8) to balance coverage and focus.
actionable: Implement a two-stage retrieval: broad retrieval + reranking, not just single best-match, to improve context quality for agent reasoning.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents

claim: Use state machines to track user progress through workflows and conditionally swap system prompts based on state, rather than including the entire workflow in one bloated prompt.
actionable: For multi-step user journeys (assessment, onboarding, etc.), store user state in a database and pull stage-specific system prompts at runtime.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents

claim: Test AI systems at turn 10 and turn 20 of a conversation, not just single interactions - context degrades and rule compliance breaks as the conversation grows.
actionable: Include multi-turn conversation tests in your eval suite; measure performance degradation as message history accumulates.
source_title: Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents
